documents.source: S. M. Prokhorov, Soviet artist, Students from a Workers’ School, poster for public display, 1928 Workers’ schools were Soviet adult-learning institutions that offered evening classes to factory wor
documents.content:
kers to
prepare them to enroll in state-run universities.

documents.source: Ya. I. Drobinskiy, member of the Soviet Communist Party, memoirs of his political detention and trial in the 1930s, recorded by a Soviet journalist during the de-Stalinization campaign of the late 1950s but never published
documents.content:
“One of the inmates in our large holding cell was an elderly peasant from a collective farm (kolkhoz). We
gathered that his son was being held in another holding cell and they were trying to make the son testify
against his father. The old man kept saving crumbs from his daily bread ration to give to his son when they
saw each other. The son must have eventually succumbed to the beatings and signed a document saying that
the father was trying to convince him to kill the kolkhoz director. The old man denied the accusation, and no
matter how hard they tried, they couldn’t convince him to admit his ‘guilt.’ He was summoned to a joint
interrogation with the son and went in determined to defend himself and tell the truth. But when he saw his
tortured son, with the bruises from the beatings clearly visible, something inside him broke and he told the
investigator: ‘It’s true, all of it, I confess, whatever my Ilyushka told you, don’t doubt it, everything is true,’
and he immediately signed the confession they had prepared for him.
A few days later, when he next saw his son on the way to the court chambers, the old man somehow
managed to get away momentarily from his guards and gave his Ilyushka the fistful of crumbs he had
collected for him. And then the son broke down, fell on his knees, and started crying, ‘Forgive me, papa,
forgive me, I betrayed you, forgive me.’ The old man started mumbling something, patted his son on the
head, told him it was OK. The guards didn’t know what to do; their emotions overwhelmed them. Even the
judges of the tribunal, who had seen it all, were shaken to their core.”

documents.source: Alexandra Kollontai, Russian Marxist and politician, essay titled “The Soviet Woman—a Full and Equal Citizen of Her Country,” published in 1946 “
documents.content:
In opening up to women access to every sphere of creative activity, our state has simultaneously ensured all
the conditions necessary for her to fulfill her natural obligation—that of being a mother bringing up her
children and being a homemaker.
From the very beginning, Soviet law recognized that motherhood is not a private matter, but the social duty
of the equal woman citizen. This fact is set in the Constitution. The Soviet Union has solved one of the most
important and complex of problems: how to make active use of female labor in any area without this harming
motherhood.
The Soviet state provides increasing assistance to mothers. Women receive state financial benefits and
maternity leave with pay before and after the birth of the child, and their job is kept open for them until they
return from leave. Large and one-parent families receive state financial benefits to help them provide for and
bring up their children. In 1945 the state paid out more than two billion rubles [Soviet currency] in such
benefits.”

documents.source: Article describing a “contention meeting,”1 published in Shenyang Daily, a regional Chinese newspaper, 1957
documents.content:
“Chang Po-sheng, head of the propaganda department of the Communist Youth League at Shenyang
University, and Huan Chen-lu, editor of the university newspaper, gave a joint speech at the meeting. The
central problem they brought up was ‘how to abolish the absolute leadership of the Party.’
Huang Chen-lu said: ‘Before the Communist revolution, the Party enjoyed high prestige, because its
connection to the people was close and strong. But since the founding of the People’s Republic [ 1949], the
Party has made itself superior to the people. More and more persons with impure motives are joining the
Party because it has become the easiest way to acquire power, influence, and money.’
Chang Po-sheng added: ‘Outwardly, we have democratic elections, but actually the Party exercises
dictatorship and the few top leaders in the Party’s Political Bureau exercise absolute power. As for freedom
of assembly, association, and publication, these are just things written in the Constitution, and the Party has
shown that the Constitution is just a scrap of paper that it does not need to observe. Citizens only have the
right to become the Party’s obedient subjects. The Party has become the emperor of China—a sacred body
that all must obey.’”
1 “Contention meetings” were Communist Party–organized mass gatherings during a brief period in 1956–1957 in
which Chinese citizens were encouraged to openly express their criticisms of the Communist Party and its leaders.

documents.source: General Office of the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party, summary report regarding food shortages, Beijing, China, 1958 “Anhui Province:
documents.content:
A total of 1.3 million people are without food supplies. Famine has forced more than 3,000
people to leave their homes.
Hebei Province: Over 474,000 families took part in a number of food riots recently. In many villages, people
have almost no food left. It is quite common for entire families to become beggars.
Shandong Province: Over 670,000 people have had their food surplus completely cut off, and over 150,000
people have been forced to flee and become beggars.
Yunnan Province: In a small number of poorer collectives, there have been quite a few food-related riots. The
causes of these food-related riots are various. First of all, local communist officials failed to discover the real
food situation in crisis areas. Some officials were afraid of making mistakes or being accused of disloyalty to
the Party; they therefore refused to believe complaints about shortages. Second, the provision plans for some
areas have fallen short, and after riots occurred, the local officials failed to pay close attention to grain
markets.”

documents.source: Xia Ping, Chinese woman member of the Communist Party, “Make Energetic Efforts to Train Women Cadres,” article published in the political magazine Hongqi (Red Banner), 1973
documents.content:
“Some of our men comrades are still not able to deal with women correctly. They claim that women’s
‘family complications’—by which they mean our domestic roles—make it very difficult to promote women
at work.
For thousands of years the basic guiding thought of the feudal landlords in China and their capitalist servants
was to treat women as slaves, to put them in the kitchen, to burden them with heavy household chores, and to
deprive them of the rights to participate in social life and political activities. When the socialist revolution
took place, one of the most important priorities of the working class was to liberate women from this
domestic slavery. It is undeniable that the establishment of the socialist system has in fact been liberating to
Chinese women, the great majority of whom now participate in the productive labor of our nation. However,
due to the persistence of old ideas and anti-women prejudice, the problem of household duties and chores
has not been completely solved. To solve this problem, it is necessary to promote the practice that men and
women must share household chores.
We also need to pay attention to some specific practices that hold women down. We should oppose early
marriage and promote late marriage. It is essential that we continue to do a good job in providing social
welfare facilities, such as health clinics, nurseries, and kindergartens.

documents.source: Richard Kosolapov, Soviet journalist, former editor in chief of the Communist Party’s journal Komunist, article in Pravda, the largest Soviet newspaper, 1983 “
documents.content:
In our society today, there have been some discussions about moving toward greater acceptance of ‘private
ownership’ and ‘private enterprise.’ These discussions are clearly unfounded, and they can be dangerous. We
have all seen how easy it is for uncommunist-like behaviors and ideas to spring up spontaneously among us
when we let our collectivist spirit grow weak or when the Party doesn’t work hard on fostering a healthy
social and educational climate. All sorts of bribe takers, thieves, grifters, and parasites stand ready to steal
the fruits of others’ labor. Their ‘entrepreneurship’ is nothing but a desire to pocket the loose kopek (penny)
of the honest Soviet citizens. Such people are especially dangerous because they, like moths, eat holes in the
fabric of socialist economic and social relations and try to draw others into their selfishly individualistic,
anti-collectivist worldview.
These advocates of more private ownership, materialism, and consumerism are enemies of the socialist state,
and the struggle against them must be uncompromising. Maintaining full central control over both
production and consumption is of utmost importance for strengthening the organic wholeness of our system.
Socialist society is a working society, not a consumer society.”
END OF DOCUMENTS FOR QUESTION 1

parts.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which communist rule transformed Soviet and/or Chinese societies in the period circa
1930–1990.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis/Claim: Responds with a historically defensible thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning about the extent to which communist rule transformed Soviet and/or Chinese societies circa 1930–1990. The thesis must either indicate a reason for the claim OR establish analytic categories. Not earned for: restating the prompt, being overgeneralized, not establishing a line of reasoning, or making a historically indefensible claim.

sample_responses.response: Communism benefited workers, peasants, and women because it abolished many of the social and economic practices that had been holding these groups down.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning: communism transformed society by benefiting specific groups AND provides a reason (abolished oppressive practices). This is a clear, minimally acceptable thesis that earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Communist rule transformed Soviet and Chinese societies in many ways, including by creating new opportunities for women, providing new educational opportunities for young people, but also by brutally repressing those whom the communists saw as challenging their rule.
sample_responses.commentary: A stronger thesis that establishes analytic categories (women's rights, education, repression) to structure the argument. Directly addresses 'extent' by acknowledging both transformation and its limits/costs.

sample_responses.response: Chinese and Soviet societies were transformed between 1930 and 1990.
sample_responses.commentary: Merely restates the prompt. Does not establish a line of reasoning — no reason, no categories, no analytical claim about HOW or TO WHAT EXTENT transformation occurred.

sample_responses.response: Communism changed Soviet and Chinese societies during the period 1930–1990.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides a historically defensible claim but does not establish a line of reasoning. Saying 'changed' without specifying how, why, or to what categories does not earn the point.

required_elements: Communism benefited workers, peasants, and women because it abolished many social and economic practices that had been holding these groups down.
alternatives.correct_example: Communism benefited workers, peasants, and women because it abolished many social and economic practices that had been holding these groups down.

required_elements: Communist rule transformed Soviet and Chinese societies by creating new opportunities for some groups, while brutally repressing those who challenged communist rule.
alternatives.correct_example: Communist rule transformed Soviet and Chinese societies by creating new opportunities for some groups, while brutally repressing those who challenged communist rule.

wrong_examples: A simple restatement of the prompt without taking a position.
A vague claim that does not establish a line of reasoning.
An overgeneralized statement not tied to the specific prompt.
common_traps: Thesis placed in the body of the essay may not be recognized — must be intro or conclusion.
Simply listing facts without making an argument does not earn this point.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describes broader historical context accurately and connects it to the argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for accurately describing a broader historical context relevant to communist rule and Soviet/Chinese societies circa 1930–1990. The context must be more than a phrase or reference, and must describe broader historical events, developments, or processes that occur before, during, or continue after the time frame. Not earned for: overgeneralized statements, irrelevant context, or passing phrases.

sample_responses.response: Marx and Engels believed that only workers could carry out the communist revolution, but Mao believed that peasants could also lead a communist revolution, adapting Marxist theory to Chinese conditions. This ideological innovation explains why communism succeeded in agrarian China and shaped the specific ways it transformed Chinese society.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes a relevant development (Mao's adaptation of Marxism) before the exam period and connects it to why communist transformation took its particular form. More than a phrase — provides elaborated context.

sample_responses.response: Communists' main goal was to improve the lives of the working class.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable contextualization per the CB guideline. Describes the ideological goals of communism in a way that is connected to the prompt, though barely more than a phrase.

sample_responses.response: Russia and China used to be great land-based empires in the nineteenth century.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not provide context relevant to the topic. The pre-communist imperial status of Russia and China, stated without elaboration or connection to communist transformation, is not sufficient context for this prompt.

sample_responses.response: Industrial capitalism arose following the Industrial Revolution.
sample_responses.commentary: A passing phrase that does not provide meaningful context for communist rule's impact on Soviet and Chinese societies. Too vague and not connected to the prompt's focus.

required_elements: Marx and Engels believed that only workers could carry out the communist revolution, but Mao believed that peasants could also lead a communist revolution, adapting Marxist theory to Chinese conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: Marx and Engels believed that only workers could carry out the communist revolution, but Mao believed that peasants could also lead a communist revolution, adapting Marxist theory to Chinese conditions.

required_elements: The development of socialist and workers' parties in the late nineteenth century laid the groundwork for communist revolutions in Russia and China.
alternatives.correct_example: The development of socialist and workers' parties in the late nineteenth century laid the groundwork for communist revolutions in Russia and China.

wrong_examples: A passing reference to a historical event without elaboration.
Context that is not relevant to the specific prompt.
An overgeneralized statement about the entire time period.
common_traps: Context must be more than a single sentence — requires elaboration.
Must be connected to the argument, not just mentioned in passing.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents: Uses the content of at least THREE documents to address the topic of the prompt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for accurately describing — rather than simply quoting — the content of at least three documents to address the topic of communist rule's impact on Soviet and/or Chinese societies. Not earned for: fewer than three documents, misinterpreting documents, simply quoting without description, or addressing documents collectively.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 shows a Soviet poster of workers going to school, illustrating how the communist government promoted mass education. Document 3 describes how Soviet women received state assistance including paid maternity leave and childcare. Document 5 reports that over a million people in Anhui Province, China, were without food supplies during famine conditions.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes — does not merely quote — the content of three documents (1, 3, 5), all of which are relevant to how communist rule affected Soviet and Chinese societies. Each description captures what the document shows, not just who wrote it.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 shows men and women going to school carrying books, papers, and rulers. Document 5 states that '1.3 million people' had to leave their homes due to famine in Anhui Province of China.
sample_responses.commentary: Only describes two documents, and the description of Document 1 is close to quoting rather than describing. Does not meet the threshold of three documents.

required_elements: Accurately describes content from at least 3 documents
Does not simply quote without describing
Content is relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Accurately describes — rather than simply quotes — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of the prompt.

wrong_examples: Using fewer than three documents.
Simply quoting document text without describing or analyzing it.
Misinterpreting a document's content.
Addressing documents collectively rather than discussing each separately.
common_traps: Description, not quotation — must explain what the document shows or argues.
Two correctly used documents does not earn either document evidence point.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents+: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using at least FOUR documents.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for supporting an argument in response to the prompt by accurately using content from at least four documents. The four documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can support sub-arguments or counterarguments. This point builds on but is independent of the 3-document evidence point.

sample_responses.response: Document 2 recounts how sons betrayed their fathers in order to survive in communist prisons, showing the profound effects of Stalin's repressions on Russian family bonds. Document 3 states that the Soviet Union 'provides increasing assistance to mothers' and keeps their jobs open 'until they return from leave,' showing that women's lives benefitted from communist policies. Document 5 reports that millions of Chinese were suffering from starvation because local officials failed to acknowledge food shortages — showing the widespread failure of communist rule. Document 7 argues that supporters of private ownership are enemies of the socialist state, showing how the Soviet state used ideological pressure to suppress economic dissent.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses content from four documents (2, 3, 5, 7) to support arguments about multiple dimensions of communist transformation — repression of families, women's gains, food policy failures, and ideological control. Each document is connected to a specific argument, not merely described.

sample_responses.response: Documents 1 through 7 all show different aspects of communist society in the Soviet Union and China.
sample_responses.commentary: Addresses documents collectively without considering each separately, and does not connect any document's content to a specific argument. Does not earn the point.

required_elements: Uses content of at least 4 documents
Connects document content to a specific argument about the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: Uses four or more documents to support a specific argument about the prompt, connecting document evidence to a claim about cause, extent, or significance.

wrong_examples: Merely describing four documents without connecting them to an argument.
Using four documents but only three in support of an argument.
common_traps: The four documents do not need to support a single argument — sub-arguments or counterarguments count.
This point builds on a3 — both are earned independently.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond Documents: Uses at least ONE piece of specific historical evidence not found in the documents.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for using at least one specific piece of historical evidence beyond that found in the documents, relevant to the extent to which communist rule transformed Soviet and/or Chinese societies circa 1930–1990. Must be specific (not a passing phrase), must not repeat information already in the documents, and must be different from evidence used for contextualization.

sample_responses.response: In the Soviet Union, the KGB was the secret police that could arrest, torture, and imprison citizens without due process, showing how communist rule used repression to maintain power and reshape Soviet society through fear.
sample_responses.commentary: The KGB is not mentioned in any of the seven documents, so this counts as evidence beyond the documents. It is specific (names the KGB), relevant to the prompt (state repression as a form of transformation), and more than a passing phrase.

sample_responses.response: The Great Leap Forward was an economic and social campaign launched by Mao Zedong from 1958 to 1962 to transform China from an agrarian economy into an industrialized society through collectivization, resulting in a catastrophic famine that killed tens of millions of people.
sample_responses.commentary: The Great Leap Forward's full scope and death toll are not in the documents (Document 5 only mentions 1958 food shortages). This provides specific beyond-document evidence relevant to communist transformation of Chinese society.

sample_responses.response: Communist rule had both positive and negative effects on society.
sample_responses.commentary: A passing phrase — not a specific piece of historical evidence. Contains no specific names, dates, policies, or events that can be distinguished from what is in the documents.

required_elements: In the Soviet Union, the KGB was the secret police that could arrest, torture, and imprison citizens without due process, showing how communist rule used repression to maintain power.
alternatives.correct_example: In the Soviet Union, the KGB was the secret police that could arrest, torture, and imprison citizens without due process, showing how communist rule used repression to maintain power.

required_elements: The Great Leap Forward was an economic and social campaign launched by Mao Zedong to transform China from an agrarian economy into an industrialized society through collectivization, resulting in a catastrophic famine.
alternatives.correct_example: The Great Leap Forward was an economic and social campaign launched by Mao Zedong to transform China from an agrarian economy into an industrialized society through collectivization, resulting in a catastrophic famine.

wrong_examples: Repeating information already found in the documents.
A vague reference to an event without specific elaboration.
Evidence outside the time period or region of the prompt.
common_traps: Evidence must be specific — general statements about the era do not qualify.
Evidence must differ from what was used for contextualization.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing: For at least TWO documents, explains how/why the document's point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining — not just identifying — how or why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt. Mere identification of who the author is, or summary of document content without addressing HAPP relevance to an argument, does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: [Doc 2]: As a former prisoner and member of the Communist Party, the author's account of Soviet prison camps is a credible eyewitness description of the political terror in the 1930s, offering firsthand evidence of how Stalinist repression transformed family and social bonds by pitting family members against each other. [Doc 5]: The fact that this is an official Central Committee report creates a bias toward minimizing the scale of famine; yet even this official document cannot conceal that millions were without food, making it powerful evidence that communist policy failures transformed Chinese society catastrophically even by the state's own admission.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains — not merely identifies — the POV of Document 2 (eyewitness credibility of a Party member turned victim) and the purpose/historical situation of Document 5 (official report that shifts blame even while confirming disaster). Both are connected to specific arguments about transformation.

sample_responses.response: Document 6 was written by Xia Ping, a female member of the Communist Party. Document 7 was written by Richard Kosolapov, a Soviet journalist.
sample_responses.commentary: Merely identifies who the authors are. Does not explain how or why their point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to any argument about communist transformation of society.

required_elements: [Document 2]: As a former prisoner and member of the Communist Party, the author's account offers credible eyewitness evidence of the political terror in the Soviet Union in the 1930s, supporting an argument about how communist rule used state violence to transform society.
alternatives.correct_example: [Document 2]: As a former prisoner and member of the Communist Party, the author's account offers credible eyewitness evidence of the political terror in the Soviet Union in the 1930s, supporting an argument about how communist rule used state violence to transform society.

required_elements: [Document 5]: The report blames local officials who were 'afraid of making mistakes or being accused of disloyalty to the Party,' effectively shifting responsibility to the local level, supporting an argument about how the Chinese Communist Party's centralization caused human suffering.
alternatives.correct_example: [Document 5]: The report blames local officials who were 'afraid of making mistakes or being accused of disloyalty to the Party,' effectively shifting responsibility to the local level, supporting an argument about how the Chinese Communist Party's centralization caused human suffering.

wrong_examples: Simply identifying the author's occupation without explaining relevance to an argument.
Summarizing document content as 'sourcing' without addressing POV/purpose/situation/audience.
Sourcing only one document.
common_traps: Must EXPLAIN relevance — not just identify who the author is.
Connect sourcing to a specific argument, not just a general observation.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrates a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for demonstrating a complex understanding of communist rule's transformation of Soviet/Chinese societies through: (1) sophisticated argumentation — explaining multiple themes/perspectives, multiple causes/effects, or insightful cross-period/cross-region connections; OR (2) effective use of evidence — using all 7 documents, sourcing at least 4 documents, or using documents and beyond-document evidence to show different perspectives. Must be more than a phrase or reference and must be part of the argument.

sample_responses.response: While women gained important formal rights under both Soviet and Chinese communism — including access to education, paid maternity leave (Doc 3), and increased political participation (Doc 6) — Documents 3 and 6 also reveal that women continued to bear the primary responsibility for household work and childcare, showing that communist transformation was uneven: it disrupted traditional gender hierarchies without fully dismantling them.
sample_responses.commentary: Demonstrates complexity by examining nuance — communist transformation genuinely improved women's formal status AND simultaneously preserved domestic gender inequality. Uses specific documents on both sides of this tension. This is the CB's own example of a nuanced argument.

sample_responses.response: Communist rule benefited some and harmed others, using Documents 1, 3, and 6 to show gains for workers and women, Documents 2 and 5 to show how repression and famine harmed populations, and Documents 4 and 7 to show how citizens resisted or criticized communist regimes even under totalitarian conditions — demonstrating that communist transformation was both real and contested.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses all 7 documents to demonstrate complex understanding of communist transformation — acknowledging that the same system produced both meaningful gains and catastrophic harms. The CB explicitly identifies this as a path to the complexity point.

sample_responses.response: Communist rule had many effects on Soviet and Chinese societies, which shows that the situation was complex.
sample_responses.commentary: States that the situation was complex without actually demonstrating complexity. A passing reference to complexity does not earn this point — substantive elaboration is required.

required_elements: Analyzing how even though women gained formal rights in both the USSR and China under communist rule, they also continued to bear the main responsibility for raising children and managing the household, showing the limits of communist transformation.
alternatives.correct_example: Analyzing how even though women gained formal rights in both the USSR and China under communist rule, they also continued to bear the main responsibility for raising children and managing the household, showing the limits of communist transformation.

required_elements: Arguing that communist rule had a multi-faceted impact on societies — benefiting some groups like workers and women in certain respects, while brutally harming others through purges, gulags, and engineered famines.
alternatives.correct_example: Arguing that communist rule had a multi-faceted impact on societies — benefiting some groups like workers and women in certain respects, while brutally harming others through purges, gulags, and engineered famines.

wrong_examples: A passing mention of complexity without substantive development.
Simply stating 'there were multiple perspectives' without elaborating.
Using only 5–6 documents and claiming complexity without other demonstration.
common_traps: Complexity must be part of the sustained argument, not tacked on at the end.
Must be more than a phrase or reference — requires substantive development.
